summary: Nadia argues that the patronage model for creators has been distorted by Kickstarter's blueprint of reward tiers and transactional perks, when what patrons actually want is intimacy and ritual. Drawing from her own experience transforming her newsletter from brief updates into rich personal dispatches, she found that giving more of herself—not less—deepened subscriber engagement. Using The Little Prince's concept of 'taming' through rituals, she explains that people follow creators not for material rewards but because regular contact creates meaningful bonds. She contends that creators sell intimacy to patrons and 'stuff' to customers, and that the industry should redesign patronage tools around relationships rather than transactions.
key_points: Nadia's newsletter engagement improved dramatically when she shifted from transactional updates to sharing her full world and interests, proving subscribers wanted intimacy not information
The Little Prince's concept of 'taming' through rituals explains why people form irrational loyalty to creators—regular contact creates unique bonds regardless of the content's objective value

Kickstarter functioned as 'original sin' for patronage platforms, establishing a blueprint of reward tiers and perks that every subsequent platform copied without questioning whether it actually served creators
The distinction between patrons and customers is that patrons pay for ritual and relationship while customers pay for stuff and perks
Modern patronage tools should be redesigned around the principle that genuine supporters don't need to be bribed to stick around
money_quotes: It turns out that the people who followed my work didn't really want 'updates'. In fact, I'm starting to suspect that people have no idea what I actually do for a living (and I kind of like it that way!).
Whether it's a newsletter, podcast, or social platform, people follow other people for reasons that are often incomprehensible, even to themselves. Somehow, your life became a ritual to them.

It's not that I or anyone else is really that gosh darn interesting. Rather, there are so many people out there to look at, and so many people they can reach, it's inevitable any one will be able to draw a small crowd.
Patrons pay for the ritual, and the ritual tames them. These are ongoing relationships, not transactions. Creators sell intimacy to patrons. They sell 'stuff' - perks - to customers.
Sometimes I think of Kickstarter as the original sin for modern patronage.
Reward tiers are an awkward thing for creators and their patrons. People don't necessarily need material perks. They just want to feel close to that person.
Like any other relationship, if someone loves and cares about your work, you don't need to bribe them to stick around.
key_insight: Patronage is fundamentally about ritual and intimacy rather than transactional perks, and the creator economy's tools should be redesigned around relationships instead of copying Kickstarter's reward-tier blueprint.
